questions.question: Your rate limiter uses a shared counter in Redis (INCR + EXPIRE). At the start of each time window, 1,000 concurrent requests all call INCR simultaneously. The first 100 should be allowed, but you observe some windows allowing 105-110 requests. What causes this?

options.text: The INCR and EXPIRE are separate commands. Between INCR setting the key and EXPIRE setting the TTL, another request can INCR the same key, creating a race. Use a Lua script to make both atomic.
Redis's single-threaded model queues the 1,000 INCRs, but some complete before the EXPIRE is set, so they don't see the TTL and create a new window early.
Network latency between the application and Redis means some INCR responses arrive after the check, so the application allows requests it should have rejected.
Redis replication lag causes read-after-write inconsistency when replicas serve stale counter values during the burst.

questions.explanation: Non-atomic INCR+EXPIRE creates a race condition; using a Lua script or INCR with EX in a single command ensures the counter and its TTL are set atomically.

questions.question: A developer writes a unit test for a UserService.createUser() method. The test connects to a real PostgreSQL database to verify the insert. Another developer argues this is wrong. Why?

options.text: Unit tests should test logic in isolation. Using a real database makes it an integration test — mock the repository layer to test the service's business logic without external dependencies.
Using a real database is fine for unit tests as long as the test database is separate from production and is reset between test runs.
The test is wrong because PostgreSQL is too slow for unit tests. Using an in-memory database like SQLite would make it a proper unit test.
Unit tests should only test private methods. Testing createUser() directly is an integration test because it's a public method with external effects.

questions.explanation: Unit tests isolate the code under test from external systems; database interactions should be mocked so the test focuses on business logic, not infrastructure.

questions.question: Your unit tests all pass, but the signup flow fails in production because the email service returns a format the code doesn't handle. What type of test would have caught this?

options.text: An integration test that tests the signup flow with the real (or realistic) email service, verifying the code correctly handles actual response formats.
A more detailed unit test that covers all possible response formats from the email service by mocking each variation.
An end-to-end test that runs the entire application in a browser and verifies the user receives the actual email.
A smoke test that checks if the email service is reachable before running the signup flow in production.

questions.explanation: Integration tests verify that components work correctly together with real dependencies, catching interface mismatches that mocked unit tests miss.

questions.question: Your API handles 100 requests/second in development. Before launching a marketing campaign expected to drive 10x traffic, your team wants to verify the system can handle the load. What should you run?

options.text: A load test that simulates 1,000+ concurrent requests per second against a production-like environment to identify bottlenecks before real traffic hits.
A stress test in production during off-peak hours to see exactly how the real infrastructure handles increased traffic.
A code review of the hot paths to estimate performance theoretically, since load testing environments never accurately reflect production.
A capacity planning document based on the database's published benchmarks and the cloud provider's SLA guarantees.

questions.explanation: Load testing in a production-like environment reveals real bottlenecks (database connections, memory limits, third-party rate limits) that theoretical analysis misses.

questions.question: The frontend team and backend team deploy independently. The backend changes a response field from 'userName' to 'user_name'. Frontend tests pass (they mock the backend), but the app breaks in production. What testing approach prevents this?

options.text: Contract testing: both teams agree on an API contract, and each side runs tests against it. The backend verifies it produces the expected format, and the frontend verifies it can consume it.
End-to-end tests that deploy both frontend and backend together and test the full flow in a staging environment before each release.
Schema validation using TypeScript types shared between frontend and backend repositories to catch field name changes at compile time.
API versioning so the backend never changes existing field names. New fields are added in a new version while old fields remain.

questions.explanation: Contract tests verify both sides adhere to a shared API specification, catching breaking changes before deployment without requiring both services to run together.

questions.question: Your codebase has 95% unit test coverage, but bugs keep appearing in production. The tests are mostly checking that methods return specific hardcoded values. What's wrong with the testing strategy?

options.text: High coverage doesn't equal quality. Tests should verify behavior and edge cases, not just that code executes. The tests are likely testing implementation details instead of meaningful assertions.
95% coverage is insufficient. You need 100% coverage including all branches and exception paths to catch production bugs.
Unit tests can't catch production bugs by design. You need integration and end-to-end tests instead of unit tests.
The tests are running against mocked dependencies, which hides real bugs. Replacing all mocks with real services would expose the issues.

questions.explanation: Code coverage measures execution, not correctness; meaningful tests assert behavior, boundary conditions, and error handling — not just that code runs without crashing.

questions.question: Your integration tests use a shared test database. Tests pass when run individually but fail when run in parallel because one test's data setup interferes with another's assertions. What's the best fix?

options.text: Wrap each test in a database transaction that rolls back after the test completes, or use isolated schemas/containers per test suite to prevent data interference.
Run tests sequentially instead of in parallel. Integration tests are inherently slow and shouldn't be parallelized.
Use unique prefixes for test data (e.g., test1_user, test2_user) so each test's data doesn't conflict with others.
Clear the entire database before each test with TRUNCATE on all tables to ensure a clean state.

questions.explanation: Transaction rollback or per-test isolation ensures each test runs against a clean, independent state without data contamination from parallel test execution.

questions.question: Your load test shows the API handles 500 requests/second with a p50 latency of 50ms. But the p99 latency is 5,000ms. The team considers the test passing because average latency is 80ms. Why is this wrong?

options.text: The p99 shows 1% of users experience 100x worse latency. Averages hide tail latency — 1 in 100 requests taking 5 seconds means many users will have a poor experience.
The p99 is an outlier caused by garbage collection pauses and can be safely ignored since it only affects 1% of requests.
Load test p99 numbers are unreliable because coordinated omission in the test tool artificially inflates tail latencies.
The p50 is the only metric that matters for user experience since most users experience median latency, not tail latency.

questions.explanation: Tail latency (p99/p999) directly impacts user experience; at 500 req/s, a 5-second p99 means 5 users per second have an unacceptable experience.

questions.question: Your API has 50 consumer services. You want to change a response field, but you're not sure which consumers depend on it. How do contract tests help here?

options.text: Consumer-driven contract tests let each consumer define what fields it needs. Before changing the API, you run all consumer contracts to see which would break.
Contract tests generate a dependency graph showing which consumers use which fields, but they can't prevent breaking changes.
Contract tests only work between two services. With 50 consumers, you'd need an API gateway to manage compatibility instead.
Contract tests verify the API schema matches an OpenAPI specification, but they don't track individual consumer dependencies on specific fields.

questions.explanation: Consumer-driven contracts capture each consumer's expectations; running them before a change reveals exactly which consumers would break, enabling safe API evolution.

questions.question: A developer mocks every dependency in their unit test — database, HTTP client, logger, clock, config loader. The test passes, but the function under test has a bug where it passes arguments to the HTTP client in the wrong order. Why didn't the test catch this?

options.text: Over-mocking means the test verifies the code's interaction with mocks, not its correctness. The mock accepts any argument order, so the bug goes undetected.
The mock should have been configured with strict argument matching. The developer used a lenient mock that accepts any arguments.
Unit tests can't verify HTTP client behavior. This bug can only be caught by integration tests against the real HTTP service.
The function has too many dependencies. Refactoring it into smaller functions with fewer mocks each would expose the argument order bug.

questions.explanation: Excessive mocking creates tests that pass regardless of actual behavior; the test verifies mock interactions rather than real logic, hiding bugs at component boundaries.

questions.question: Your integration test suite takes 45 minutes to run. Developers skip it locally and only run it in CI, where it blocks merges for nearly an hour. What strategy reduces this without sacrificing coverage?

options.text: Parallelize tests across multiple containers, use test impact analysis to run only tests affected by changed code, and split the suite into fast critical-path tests and slower comprehensive tests.
Replace integration tests with unit tests using mocks to achieve the same coverage in seconds instead of minutes.
Run integration tests only nightly instead of per-commit, accepting that bugs may be discovered hours after the code is merged.
Move all integration tests to production monitoring. If a deployment breaks something, automated rollback handles it faster than blocking CI.

questions.explanation: Parallelization, test impact analysis, and tiered test suites reduce feedback time while maintaining comprehensive integration coverage.

questions.question: Your load test runs from a single machine with 10,000 concurrent virtual users. Results show 2,000 req/s throughput, but production handles 5,000 req/s easily. The test machine's CPU is at 100%. What's wrong with the test?

options.text: The load generator itself is the bottleneck. A single machine can't generate enough load, so the test measures the generator's capacity, not the server's. Distribute the load across multiple machines.
The test is running over a local network with lower bandwidth than production. Using the same cloud region as production would give accurate results.
10,000 virtual users is too many for a single test. Reduce to 1,000 users and extrapolate the results linearly to estimate 10,000-user performance.
The load test tool is inefficient and uses one thread per virtual user. Switching to an async load generator would allow more virtual users on the same machine.

questions.explanation: When the load generator is CPU-saturated, it becomes the bottleneck; distributed load generation from multiple machines ensures the test accurately measures server capacity.

questions.question: Your team uses Pact for consumer-driven contract testing. The provider passes all contract tests but adds a new required field ('tax_amount') to the CreateOrder request. Existing consumers don't send this field and start failing in production. Why didn't contract tests catch this?

options.text: Consumer-driven contracts only verify what consumers expect, not what the provider requires. The new required field was a provider-side change that no existing consumer contract covered.
Pact contracts are generated from consumer mocks, not real requests. The mocks didn't include the new field because they were written before the change.
Contract tests only validate response schemas, not request schemas. Request validation requires a separate provider-driven contract approach.
The provider's contract verification should have failed because it received requests without the required field, but the test framework auto-generated the missing field.

questions.explanation: Consumer-driven contracts reflect what consumers send and expect; adding a new required field on the provider side is a backward-incompatible change that existing consumer contracts won't test for.

questions.question: Your production logs contain thousands of 'User logged in' messages per minute, making it hard to find error logs during an incident. The debug and info logs drown out warnings and errors. What should you do?

options.text: Set production log level to WARN or ERROR so only actionable log entries are recorded. Use DEBUG and INFO levels only in development or when temporarily diagnosing a specific issue.
Keep all log levels but route them to separate files (info.log, error.log) so error logs are easy to find during incidents.
Remove all INFO-level log statements from the codebase since they provide no value in production and just generate noise.
Add a log aggregation tool like ELK that lets you filter by log level during incidents without changing what's emitted.

questions.explanation: Setting the production log level to WARN/ERROR reduces noise and ensures logs contain only actionable information during incidents.

questions.question: Your team monitors the API by checking if the health endpoint returns 200 every 30 seconds. During an outage, the health check still passes, but users can't complete purchases. What metric is missing?

options.text: Application-level metrics like request latency, error rates, and throughput per endpoint. A passing health check doesn't mean the application is functioning correctly for users.
Infrastructure metrics like CPU and memory usage, which would show the server is overloaded even when the health endpoint responds.
A more comprehensive health endpoint that checks database connectivity, external service availability, and queue depth before returning 200.
Synthetic transaction monitoring that simulates a real purchase every minute and alerts if it fails, replacing the simple health check.

questions.explanation: Application metrics (latency, errors, throughput) reflect real user experience; a healthy status endpoint doesn't guarantee that business-critical flows are working.

questions.question: A user reports that checkout takes 15 seconds. Your system has 6 microservices involved in checkout. Server logs show each service responds quickly. You can't tell where the 15 seconds is spent. What observability tool helps?

options.text: Distributed tracing (e.g., Jaeger, Zipkin) that tracks a request across all services, showing the time spent in each service and the gaps between them.
A centralized log aggregator that correlates logs from all 6 services by timestamp to reconstruct the request timeline.
APM (Application Performance Monitoring) installed on each service to profile CPU and memory usage during the checkout flow.
Network packet capture (tcpdump) between services to measure the actual network latency for each inter-service call.

questions.explanation: Distributed tracing propagates a trace ID across services, creating a visual timeline that shows exactly where time is spent in a multi-service request flow.

questions.question: Your monitoring system sends an alert for every 5xx error. The on-call engineer gets 200 alerts per night for transient errors that resolve themselves. They start ignoring alerts. What should you change?

options.text: Alert on sustained error rate thresholds (e.g., >5% of requests returning 5xx for 5 minutes) instead of individual errors, reducing noise while catching real outages.
Reduce the alert severity from 'critical' to 'warning' so the on-call engineer is notified but not paged for transient errors.
Add auto-remediation that restarts the service on every 5xx error, eliminating the need for human intervention on transient issues.
Route transient error alerts to a Slack channel instead of paging the on-call engineer, so they can review them asynchronously.

questions.explanation: Threshold-based alerting eliminates noise from transient errors while ensuring sustained issues trigger actionable alerts that the team won't ignore.

questions.question: Your application logs unstructured text like 'Order 123 placed by user 456 at 2025-03-20'. Querying these logs to find all orders by user 456 requires regex parsing. What logging improvement makes this easier?

options.text: Use structured logging (JSON format) with discrete fields like {"event": "order_placed", "order_id": 123, "user_id": 456} so log aggregators can index and query by any field.
Standardize the text format across all services (e.g., 'ORDER|123|456|2025-03-20') so regex patterns are simpler and more reliable.
Write log entries to a relational database table with columns for order_id, user_id, and timestamp instead of text files.
Add a log parsing pipeline (like Logstash) that extracts fields from unstructured text and stores them as structured data in Elasticsearch.

questions.explanation: Structured logging emits machine-parseable records (typically JSON) with named fields, enabling efficient querying, filtering, and aggregation without regex.

questions.question: Your dashboard shows average API response time as 200ms. Users complain about slowness. You discover the average is skewed by the 90% of requests that complete in 50ms, hiding the 10% that take 2 seconds. What metric better represents this?

options.text: Use percentile metrics (p95, p99) instead of averages. The p95 would show that 5% of requests take over 2 seconds, accurately reflecting the slow experience many users have.
Use the median (p50) instead of the average, since it's more resistant to outliers and better represents the 'typical' user experience.
Create separate dashboards for fast and slow requests by splitting at a 500ms threshold, so each group has a meaningful average.
Use a weighted average that gives more importance to slow requests, since they have a disproportionate impact on user satisfaction.

questions.explanation: Percentiles (p95, p99) reveal tail latency that averages hide; they show the worst experience a significant portion of users actually encounters.

questions.question: Your distributed tracing shows that a request takes 3 seconds total: Service A takes 500ms, Service B takes 500ms, and Service C takes 500ms. But 500ms + 500ms + 500ms = 1,500ms, not 3,000ms. Where is the missing 1.5 seconds?

options.text: The missing time is likely in network latency between services, queue wait times, or connection pool acquisition — the gaps between spans that aren't captured by service-level instrumentation.
The trace collector is adding processing overhead at each span boundary, contributing 500ms of overhead per service.
The services' clocks are out of sync, causing spans to appear offset in the trace timeline and inflating the total duration.
Service B and C are running concurrently (not sequentially), so the trace shows their durations stacked rather than overlapped.

questions.explanation: Time between spans reveals infrastructure overhead — network latency, DNS lookups, load balancer routing, and connection pool waits that occur between instrumented service logic.

questions.question: Your alerting system fires when CPU exceeds 80%. After several false alarms where high CPU was caused by legitimate traffic spikes (and the system handled it fine), the team wants better alerts. What should you alert on instead?

options.text: Alert on user-facing symptoms (error rate, latency SLO breaches) rather than infrastructure metrics. High CPU that doesn't impact users isn't an actionable alert.
Raise the CPU threshold to 95% so only truly critical CPU saturation triggers an alert.
Add auto-scaling so CPU never stays above 80%, eliminating the alert condition entirely.
Alert on CPU rate of change (derivative) rather than absolute value, so gradual increases from traffic are ignored but sudden spikes trigger alerts.

questions.explanation: Symptom-based alerting (latency, errors, SLO breaches) triggers on actual user impact, avoiding false alarms from resource metrics that don't always correlate with degradation.

questions.question: Your service logs contain sensitive user data (emails, phone numbers, SSNs) in request payloads. A compliance audit flags this as a PII violation. Removing all logging would hurt debugging. What's the right approach?

options.text: Implement log redaction that masks or removes PII fields before they're written to logs, preserving debugging context while protecting sensitive data.
Encrypt all log entries using AES-256 so PII is protected at rest. Decryption keys are held by the security team for incident review.
Store logs containing PII in a separate, access-controlled log stream with a short retention period (7 days) to limit exposure.
Switch to logging only request IDs and correlation IDs. When debugging, use the ID to look up the original request in an encrypted audit database.

questions.explanation: Log redaction strips or masks PII before log storage, balancing operational debugging needs with regulatory compliance requirements.

questions.question: Your Prometheus metrics show a counter 'http_requests_total' with labels for method, path, status, and user_id. With 100K users, Prometheus runs out of memory. What's the problem?

options.text: High cardinality. Using user_id as a label creates 100K unique time series per path/method/status combination. Labels should have bounded, low-cardinality values.
Prometheus can't handle more than 10K unique label values per metric. You need to shard across multiple Prometheus instances.
Counter metrics with many labels are inefficient. Switching to a gauge or histogram would reduce memory usage per time series.
The scrape interval is too frequent, causing Prometheus to store too many data points per time series in its TSDB.

questions.explanation: High-cardinality labels (like user_id) explode the number of unique time series, exhausting Prometheus's memory; use logs or traces for per-user data instead.

questions.question: Your service processes events from a Kafka topic. You add distributed tracing, but traces end at the Kafka producer and new traces start at the consumer. There's no link between the two. How do you connect them?

options.text: Propagate the trace context (trace ID and span ID) as Kafka message headers so the consumer can continue the same trace, linking the producer span to the consumer span.
Use the Kafka offset as the trace ID so both producer and consumer reference the same identifier for correlation.
Install a tracing plugin on the Kafka broker that automatically links producer and consumer spans using the broker's internal request tracking.
Batch trace export from both producer and consumer to the same collector, which merges traces with matching timestamps automatically.

questions.explanation: Injecting trace context (W3C Trace Context or similar) into message headers lets consumers extract and continue the trace, creating an end-to-end view across async boundaries.

questions.question: Your team defines an SLO of 99.9% availability (43.8 minutes of downtime per month). You want to alert before the budget is exhausted. A traditional threshold alert (e.g., >1% errors for 5 min) fires too late. What approach works better?

options.text: Use burn rate alerting: calculate how fast the error budget is being consumed. If the current rate would exhaust the monthly budget in hours instead of days, fire an alert early.
Set a tighter SLO (99.99%) internally so the team has buffer, and alert when the public SLO (99.9%) is at risk.
Alert when accumulated downtime reaches 50% of the monthly budget (21.9 minutes), giving the team the remaining half to fix the issue.
Use anomaly detection with machine learning to predict SLO violations before they happen, alerting on predicted future error rates.

questions.explanation: Burn rate alerts measure how quickly the error budget is being consumed relative to the time remaining, providing early warning proportional to the severity of the incident.

questions.question: Your Docker image is 2.5 GB. Pulling it during deployment takes 4 minutes per server. What's the most effective way to reduce the image size?

options.text: Use a multi-stage build with a minimal base image (like Alpine or distroless) so the final image only contains the compiled application and its runtime dependencies.
Compress the image with gzip before pushing to the registry so it takes less time to transfer over the network.
Split the application into multiple smaller containers so each one is under 500 MB and can be pulled faster.
Use a local image cache on each server so the image is only pulled once, and subsequent deployments use the cached version.

questions.explanation: Multi-stage builds separate build dependencies from runtime, and minimal base images (Alpine/distroless) drastically reduce the final image size.

questions.question: Developers merge code to main and manually deploy to staging by running a deploy script on their laptop. Occasionally, someone forgets to run tests first, and broken code reaches staging. What should you implement?

options.text: A CI/CD pipeline that automatically runs tests on every push to main and deploys to staging only if tests pass, removing the manual step entirely.
A pre-merge code review requirement so another developer verifies the code works before it's merged to main.
A pre-push Git hook that runs tests locally before allowing the push, preventing untested code from reaching the repository.
A staging environment that automatically rolls back deployments if the health check fails within 5 minutes of deployment.

questions.explanation: A CI/CD pipeline automates testing and deployment, ensuring every code change is tested before reaching staging and removing error-prone manual steps.

questions.question: Your single web server can handle 1,000 requests/second. Marketing expects a campaign to drive 5,000 requests/second. Upgrading to a bigger server (vertical scaling) is expensive. What's the alternative?

options.text: Add more identical server instances behind a load balancer (horizontal scaling) to distribute the 5,000 req/s across multiple machines.
Optimize the application code to handle 5x more requests on the same server, since horizontal scaling adds networking complexity.
Use a CDN to cache all API responses so the origin server never receives more than 1,000 req/s regardless of total traffic.
Queue incoming requests and process them at the server's 1,000 req/s pace, accepting higher latency during the campaign.

questions.explanation: Horizontal scaling distributes load across multiple identical instances behind a load balancer, providing cost-effective capacity without the limits of a single machine.

questions.question: Your team deploys a new application version that expects a 'phone_number' column in the users table, but the database migration hasn't run yet. The app crashes on startup with 'column not found.' What deployment practice prevents this?

options.text: Run database migrations as a separate step before deploying the new application version, ensuring the schema is ready when the new code starts.
Make the application check for the column at startup and create it automatically if it doesn't exist, removing the dependency on manual migrations.
Use a schema-less database (like MongoDB) so the application can write new fields without requiring migration scripts.
Deploy the application and migration simultaneously in the same transaction so they either both succeed or both fail.

questions.explanation: Running database migrations before application deployment ensures the schema matches what the new code expects, preventing startup failures.

questions.question: Your Dockerfile installs dependencies with `npm install` and then copies the application code. Every time you change a line of code, Docker reinstalls all dependencies (3 minutes). How do you speed this up?

options.text: Copy package.json and package-lock.json first, run npm install, then copy the rest of the code. Docker caches the dependency layer and only rebuilds when package files change.
Mount node_modules as a Docker volume so dependencies are shared between the host and container and never need reinstalling.
Use npm ci instead of npm install because it's faster and skips the resolution step by using the lockfile directly.
Add node_modules to .dockerignore and install dependencies at container startup instead of during the build, so the image stays small.

questions.explanation: Docker layer caching means unchanged layers are reused; copying package files and installing dependencies before copying code ensures npm install only reruns when dependencies actually change.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs unit tests, integration tests, builds the Docker image, runs security scans, and deploys — all sequentially. The total pipeline takes 40 minutes. Developers complain about slow feedback. What optimization has the biggest impact?

options.text: Run independent stages in parallel (e.g., unit tests + security scan simultaneously) and fail fast — stop the pipeline immediately when any stage fails instead of running all remaining stages.
Skip integration tests in the CI pipeline and run them nightly instead, since they're the slowest stage.
Use a faster CI runner with more CPU cores so each stage completes faster without changing the pipeline structure.
Cache the Docker image layers across pipeline runs so the build stage only processes changed layers.

questions.explanation: Parallelizing independent stages and failing fast dramatically reduces pipeline duration and gives developers quicker feedback on failures.

questions.question: Your application scales horizontally, but it stores user upload files on the local filesystem of each server. After scaling to 3 instances, users intermittently can't find their uploaded files. Why?

options.text: Each server has its own filesystem. A file uploaded to Server 1 isn't accessible from Server 2 or 3. Use shared storage (S3, NFS) so all instances access the same files.
The load balancer's round-robin algorithm routes users to different servers. Use sticky sessions so each user always hits the server holding their files.
The filesystem writes are buffered and not flushed to disk immediately. Adding explicit fsync after each upload ensures files are persisted before the response is sent.
The upload endpoint is hitting a race condition where two servers try to write the same filename simultaneously. Adding a file lock prevents corruption.

questions.explanation: Local filesystems are not shared between instances; using external object storage (like S3) ensures uploaded files are accessible from any server.

questions.question: You need to rename a database column from 'name' to 'full_name' with zero downtime. The old code reads 'name' and the new code reads 'full_name'. A direct rename would break the old code during the rolling deployment. What's the safe approach?

options.text: Use an expand-and-contract migration: first add 'full_name' and backfill it, deploy code that reads both, then drop 'name' after all instances use the new column.
Rename the column in a single migration and deploy the new code simultaneously, using a maintenance window of under 1 second.
Create a database view that aliases 'name' as 'full_name' so both old and new code can query the view without breaking.
Use a feature flag to switch all instances from reading 'name' to 'full_name' atomically, then rename the column.

questions.explanation: Expand-and-contract (parallel change) allows old and new code to coexist during rolling deployments by maintaining both columns until the migration is complete.

questions.question: Your containerized app runs as root inside the Docker container. A vulnerability in the app allows an attacker to escape the container and gain root access to the host. What should you change?

options.text: Run the container process as a non-root user by adding USER in the Dockerfile. This limits the blast radius if the container is compromised.
Use --privileged flag to give the container full host capabilities, which enables kernel-level security features that aren't available to unprivileged containers.
Run the container in a VM-based runtime (like Kata Containers) that provides hardware-level isolation between the container and the host.
Drop all Linux capabilities with --cap-drop=ALL. This prevents the root user inside the container from performing any privileged operations on the host.

questions.explanation: Running as non-root inside the container follows the principle of least privilege, reducing the risk and impact of container escape vulnerabilities.

questions.question: Your team deploys 10 times per day. A bad deployment causes a 30-minute outage before the team notices. You want to detect and roll back bad deploys within 2 minutes. What deployment strategy achieves this?

options.text: Canary deployments with automated rollback: route 5% of traffic to the new version, monitor error rates and latency, and automatically roll back if metrics degrade beyond thresholds.
Blue-green deployment with a 2-minute smoke test: deploy to the green environment, run automated tests, then switch traffic from blue to green.
Feature flags that disable new code paths if error rates spike, without requiring a full deployment rollback.
Deployment windows limited to low-traffic hours so outages affect fewer users and give the team more time to react.

questions.explanation: Canary deployments limit blast radius by exposing only a small percentage of traffic, and automated metric-based rollback ensures bad deploys are reverted in minutes.

questions.question: Your auto-scaler adds new instances when CPU exceeds 70%. But traffic spikes so suddenly that by the time new instances are ready (3 minutes to boot), the existing instances are already overwhelmed and returning 503s. What's the solution?

options.text: Use predictive auto-scaling based on historical traffic patterns to pre-provision instances before expected spikes, combined with a warm pool of pre-initialized standby instances for unexpected bursts.
Lower the CPU threshold to 40% so scaling triggers earlier, giving instances more headroom before they're overwhelmed.
Switch to serverless functions that scale instantly with no boot time, eliminating the cold-start scaling gap.
Over-provision by running 3x the baseline capacity at all times so spikes never exceed available resources.

questions.explanation: Predictive scaling anticipates demand from patterns, and warm pools keep pre-booted instances ready, together eliminating the gap between traffic spikes and capacity availability.

questions.question: Your production database has 500 million rows. A migration needs to add a NOT NULL column with a default value. Running ALTER TABLE on the live database locks the table for 20 minutes, causing an outage. How do you handle this safely?

options.text: Use an online schema change tool (like pt-online-schema-change or gh-ost) that creates a shadow table, copies data in batches, and swaps tables atomically — all without locking the original table.
Add the column as NULLable first (instant in PostgreSQL 11+), backfill the default in batches, then add the NOT NULL constraint separately once all rows have values.
Schedule the migration during a maintenance window at 3 AM when traffic is minimal, accepting 20 minutes of downtime.
Both A and B are valid approaches — A works for MySQL and B leverages PostgreSQL-specific optimizations. The right choice depends on the database engine.

questions.explanation: Online schema change tools (MySQL) and staged NULLable-then-constrain migrations (PostgreSQL) are both valid strategies for large-table migrations without downtime; the best approach depends on the database.
